1
Outage Performance Analysis of RIS-FA Assisted NOMA Systems
over Nakagami-m Fading Channels
Haiying Chen, Xiaoping Jin, Member, IEEE, Weidong Chen, Yao Ge, Member, IEEE,
Meiyan Song, Member, IEEE, Jianrong Bao, Senior Member, IEEE, Chongwen Huang, Member, IEEE
and Yudong Yao, Fellow, IEEE
AbstractÐFluid antenna (FA) is an emerging technology in
recent years to switch physical locations of antennas in a
predetermined small space. This paper proposes a reconﬁg-
urable intelligent surfaces (RIS)-cooperative framework with FA-
assisted non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) system, namely
FA-RIS-NOMA. Targeting multi-interference scenarios in urban
environments, the considered system incorporates a base station
(BS) equipped with a conventional antenna and users each
equipped with an FA. Additionally, the RIS is utilized to forward
signals between BS and users blocked by obstacles. The non-
line-of-sight multipath fading characteristics of the RIS-assisted
link are modeled as a Nakagami-m channel. To overcome the
multiuser interference and improve the system performance, we
develop the NOMA technique for resource allocation. Meanwhile,
to reduce the signal processing complexity at the receiver, a group
optimization greedy detection method is proposed. To evaluate
the system’s reliability, the outage probability for each user is
analyzed by using the copula function. This method enables the
derivation of the cumulative distribution and probability density
functions for the equivalent user-side channel, from which closed-
form outage probability expressions are obtained. Numerical
results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves signal-
to-noise ratio gains of approximately 9 dB and 8 dB over ﬁxed-
antenna and relay-assisted systems, respectively. Furthermore,
the proposed detection method reduces computational complexity
by over 75% with less than 0.5 dB performance degradation.
Index TermsÐFluid antenna, reconﬁgurable intelligent sur-

2024YW39. (Corresponding author: Xiaoping Jin.)

faces, non-orthogonal multiple access, Nakagami-m channel,
group optimization greedy detection, outage probability.
I. INTRODUCTION
W
ITH the rapid development of wireless communica-
tions networks, multi-input multiple-output (MIMO)
technology has signiﬁcantly improved spectral efﬁciency and
reliability in communications. In MIMO technology, in order
to achieve the desired diversity gain, it is usually required
to deploy multiple antennas with a spacing of at least half
a wavelength between them [1]. However, the development
of integrated circuits and the Internet of Things (IoT) has
driven a trend toward device miniaturization. This trend makes
it difﬁcult to accommodate sufﬁcient antennas for MIMO
technology, severely limiting communication performance. In
recent years, ﬂuid antenna (FA) has become a potential so-
lution to address this problem by quickly switching between
different positions through a single antenna aperture.
FA refers to a software-controllable ﬂuid possessing a con-
ductive radiating structure. Reconﬁguration of this structure’s
shape or position alters polarization, operating frequency,
radiation pattern. It also encompasses designs independent of
the ﬂuid material itself [2]. The most distinctive structural
feature of FA is its concept of ports. These are L ﬁxed
positions uniformly distributed along a linear dimension. A
mechanically ﬂexible antenna element switches to the port
receiving the strongest signal, constituting optimal port se-
lection [3]. This mechanism underscores a critical advantage
of FA: superior hardware efﬁciency. Unlike traditional MIMO
systems that mandate a dedicated radio frequency (RF) chain
for each antenna element, FA achieves high-resolution spatial
diversity using only a single RF chain. The authors in [4]
stated that FA can support a large number of ports despite
its small size. This is equivalent to obtaining diversity gain
through numerous spatially distributed ports. Such unique
structure of FA inevitably introduces port-to-port correlation.
This correlation depends on the number of ports and antenna
size, and it is typically modeled by using the Bessel function
[5]. The dynamic characteristics of FA are highly suitable
for modern terminal requirements. Particularly, its ability to
suppress deep fading continues driving academic research
interest. This has led to notable breakthroughs, such as those
in [6]±[8]. Notably, thanks to the reconﬁgurability of FA, port
switching has now become a nearly instantaneous electronic
process, eliminating latency bottlenecks [9].
Complicated urban wireless environments face signiﬁ-
cant challenges from dense obstacle-induced non-line-of-sight
This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Internet of Things Journal. This is the author's version which has not been fully edited and
content may change prior to final publication. Citation information: DOI 10.1109/JIOT.2026.3668981

Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 30,2026 at 07:05:46 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
2
(NLOS) transmission and co-channel interference. Achieving
more stable communications remains a key research direction.
Early solutions mainly relied on active relaying. Speciﬁcally,
the works in [10] and [11] proposed to deploy dedicated re-
peaters between base station (BS) and user equipment (UE) to
reconstruct the link. Inspired by this, [12] utilized relay users
instead of traditional repeaters for signal forwarding, while
[13] explored UAVs as dynamic airborne relay platforms.
Although these schemes alleviate the blocking problem to a
certain extent, they are constrained by inherent drawbacks such
as high hardware cost and continuous energy consumption.
In addition, maintenance complexity leads to limited practical
deployment feasibility and insufﬁcient practicality. In recent
years, the rise of reconﬁgurable intelligent surfaces (RIS)
technology has provided a new paradigm to break through this
bottleneck, as veriﬁed in the [14]±[16]. The passive nature of
RIS makes it both low-power and maintenance-free. Further-
more, [17] demonstrated efﬁcient establishment of virtual line-
of-sight (LOS) links through phase-shift matrix optimization
and evaluated the bit error rate (BER) performance. This
approach signiﬁcantly improves channel reliability in NLOS
environments. Such a disruptive passive relay mechanism is
gradually becoming a core technology to cope with dense
urban obstacle scenarios.
RIS, as passive devices, possess advantages in both low-cost
deployment and dynamic beam regulation. This effectively
mitigates adverse effects like harsh propagation environments
and wireless channel fading at the receiver, thus signiﬁcantly
enhancing channel reliability [18]. The small size of RIS en-
ables installation on high-rise building facades. This achieves
signiﬁcant space savings with minimal spatial footprint, a
feasibility conﬁrmed in [19]. Although emerging technologies
such as active RIS [20] and STAR-RIS [21] have been
proposed to mitigate the double-fading effect, they typically
require additional power consumption for signal ampliﬁcation.
In contrast, this work focuses on the passive RIS architecture
to maintain high energy efﬁciency and hardware simplicity,
which remains a vital solution for cost-effective massive
deployment. [22] established a foundational analysis of power
scaling laws in RIS-assisted MIMO systems using statistical
channel state information (CSI) and optimized phase shifts.
While instrumental, it did not examine RIS-FA coordination or
address key issues in dense urban scenariosÐsuch as intense
multipath fading and instantaneous CSI acquisition. To tackle
this issue, we propose a joint FA port selection and RIS
scheme to enhance diversity gain and outage performance.
Deep integration of RIS and FA is thus a promising direction
to overcome existing bottlenecks and leverage their comple-
mentary strengths, offering both theoretical and practical value
for further exploration.
In multiuser communication scenarios, multiple access
(MA) technology serves as a key resource allocation mech-
anism. Its evolution has been driven by two primary
goals: improving system capacity and ensuring user fairness.
While traditional orthogonal multiple access (OMA) provides
interference-free transmission via the orthogonal partitioning
of resources, its inherent exclusivity imposes a fundamental
constraint on spectral efﬁciency. Furthermore, cell-edge users
frequently require a substantial amount of resources over
extended durations to sustain communication links. This re-
source occupancy pattern signiﬁcantly reduces overall spectral
efﬁciency and exacerbates resource allocation inequity. Non-
orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) has therefore attracted
signiﬁcant attention due to its disruptive power-domain mul-
tiplexing concept. This technology is regarded as the key to
breaking through the aforementioned limitations. At its core,
it allows signals from different users to share the same block
of time-frequency resources, but differentiated via distinct
power tiers. In the downlink, BS actively transmits multiple
users’ signals in superposition. Users are categorized into
ªstrong usersº and ªweak usersº based on channel conditions,
and relies on the successive interference cancellation (SIC)
technology for demodulation. More importantly, NOMA can
prioritize weak users with higher transmit power via power
allocation strategies. It thus enhances the overall system
throughput while ensuring proportional fairness among users.
The great potential of this technique has attracted extensive
research and been gradually applied to FA. For instance, in the
context of NOMA optimization, S. Jiao et al. explored ways to
enhance the robustness of the SIC process [23], and A. Nasser
et al. focused on the synergistic optimization of user pairing
and power allocation [24]. For the innovative application of
FA systems, K.-K. Wong et al. proposed a new framework
incorporating NOMA ideas, which involves designing ﬂuid
antenna multiple access (FAMA). Based on this, they studied
two key architectures, s-FAMA and f-FAMA, in depth [25]
and [26]. Collectively, these works point out an important
research direction. This direction includes further exploring
the potential of NOMA in emerging system architectures and
promoting research on its integration with advanced antenna
technologies such as FA.
Building upon existing research on the integration of
NOMA and FA, the rational selection of channel models
remains crucial for system performance analysis, with substan-
tial potential for in-depth exploration. While most FA studies
adopt Rician fading channels, reference [27] incorporates the
Nakagami-m channel into FA research, primarily addressing
the core constraint of deploying multiple antennas in space-
limited devices within conventional antenna diversity systems.
By dynamically selecting the optimal port, this approach
signiﬁcantly reduces outage probability, thereby expanding the
scope of channel models applicable to FA systems.
This study employs the Nakagami-m channel model not
only for its effectiveness in characterizing the multipath char-
acteristics of reﬂective links in RIS-assisted systems, but also
for its ﬂexibility via the shape parameter to adapt to diverse
scenariosÐfrom severe fading to near-line-of-sight propaga-
tion. More importantly, it provides a uniﬁed framework for
analyzing the synergy among FA dynamic port selection, RIS
phase cancellation, and NOMA power allocation. This method
particularly reveals the closed-loop mechanism of the three
elements in spatially correlated fading environments, laying a
theoretical foundation for evaluating system robustness under
complex propagation conditions. Although the Nakagami-m
channel has been applied in FA research, its application
and in-depth analysis in RIS-assisted NOMA systems remain

3
underexplored. To clarify the innovation of this work, Table
I compares the core technical characteristics of existing liter-
ature with this study, where OP denotes outage probability,
MA represents multiple access, and LCD stands for low-
complexity detection.
TABLE I
COMPARISON OF CORE TECHNICAL FEATURES IN EXISTING LITERATURE
AND THIS WORK
Ref FA NOMA RIS Nakagami OP MA LCD
[28] ✓ ✓ × ✓ ✓ ✓ ×
[29] ✓ × × × ✓ × ×
[30] ✓ × ✓ × ✓ × ×

[32] ✓ ✓ × × × ✓ ×
[33] × ✓ ✓ × × ✓ ×
This work ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓
The main work and contributions of this paper are summa-
rized as follows:
• This paper establishes an FA-RIS-NOMA system. The
BS transmits signals to FA-equipped users aided by RIS,
considering only the NLOS path due to obstacles. The
BS-RIS link lacks a LOS path and is dominated by
numerous independent scatterers; therefore the Rayleigh
fading model is adopted. The RIS-UE link, characterized
by non-uniform scattering and potential dominant multi-
path components, is modeled by Nakagami-m fading for
ﬂexibility. RIS employs ideal phase shifts to perfectly
compensate the cascaded link phase. Users apply SIC for
multi-user decoding.
• The outage probability of each user is calculated. First,
the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of each FA
port is obtained based on the summed path gains of
the cascaded channel. Second, addressing the spatial
correlation of FA ports, a multivariate joint distribution
of all port strengths is established. Since FA selects
the optimal port, outage probability analysis becomes
equivalent to solving the CDF of the maximum value
in this distribution. Finally, leveraging the inverse error
function and accounting for SIC procedures, closed-form
outage probabilities are derived.
• A greedy detector based on two-port selection is proposed
and veriﬁed. Traditional FA usually uses single-port Max-
imum Likelihood (ML) detection for port selection. Based
on this, this paper develops a dual-port selection-based
FA with group optimization greedy detection (GPGD) to
reduce the complexity of the system. Numerical com-
plexity analysis demonstrates that this low-complexity
detector reduces the computational complexity by more
than 75%.
• The proposed system is validated through Monte Carlo
simulations. The results demonstrate the superior perfor-
mance of the FA-RIS-NOMA model and the dual-port
selection scheme relative to existing technologies, which
is attributed to the effective coordination between RIS
and NOMA. Such enhancement conﬁrms the system’s
robustness and practical potential in complicated wireless
environments.
The structure of this paper is organized as follows: Section
II presents the system model, channel characteristics, and
performance metrics. Section III derives the outage probability
expressions for two users with theoretical analysis. Section
IV introduces the developed low-complexity detector. Section
V provides simulation results and discussions. Section VI
corresponds to the conclusion of this paper.
Notation: Throughout this article, the following mathemat-
ical notations are adopted: Σ denotes summation; ∈ represents
set membership; C
x×y
indicates the space of x × y complex-
valued matrices; | · | stands for the modulus operator; J
0
(·)
refers to the zero-order Bessel function of the ﬁrst kind; Γ( · )
and γ(·, ·) denote the complete and lower incomplete gamma
functions, respectively; and ⌊·⌋ represents the ﬂoor function.
II. SYSTEM MODEL
As shown in Fig. 1, in this paper, we consider a downlink
RIS-FA assisted NOMA wireless communication system. The
system consists of BS, RIS, FA and multiple UEs. The BS
is equipped with a single ﬁxed-position conventional antenna,
the RIS contains N reﬂection units and the UE carries the FA
with L ports distributed along one-dimensional linear space
and supporting dynamic switching. To simplify the analysis
and highlight the core mechanisms, this paper considers only
two UEs. Considering the actual situation of the wireless
communication environment in the city, it is assumed that there
are obstacles in the direct link between the BS and the UE, i.e.,
the signals sent from the BS cannot be directly transmitted to
UE. In order to solve this challenge, this paper employs RIS
as a medium to reconnect the signal transmission between the
BS and the UE.
Fig. 1. Block diagram of the FA-assisted RIS-aided NOMA system.

.
B. Characteristics of the Channel Model
In this paper, we separate the cascaded channel into two
hops: the BS-RIS link and the RIS-UE link. To accurately
capture the distinct propagation environments of these links,
we adopt a heterogeneous channel conﬁguration.
The BS-RIS link in the ﬁrst hop is modeled as Rayleigh
fading, reﬂecting typical deployment conditions where BS
and RIS are positioned at elevated sites. In such setups, the
propagation environment exhibits rich multipath scattering and
generally lacks a dominant LOS path due to obstructions.
Therefore, assuming the channel envelope follows a Rayleigh
distribution is a reasonable and widely accepted approach.
In contrast, for the second-hop RIS-UE link, we adopt
the more general Nakagami-m fading model. This is because
user terminals are typically located in complex ground-level
environments where propagation paths involve non-uniform
scattering and varying fading severities. The Nakagami-m
model offers a ﬂexible shape parameter m that captures diverse
practical scenariosÐfrom severe fading to LOS-dominant
propagationÐthus providing a more precise representation of
the user-side channel than the conventional Rayleigh assump-
tion.
The probability density function (PDF) of the channel gain
at the BS-RIS link is represented as follows:

C. FA Correlation Model
According to the speciﬁc structure of FA, the distance
between ports is very close, resulting in correlation between
the ports of FA. Therefore, there is an intrinsic correlation
in the UE. In this paper, the correlation is analyzed using
the Jakes’ model, which is a classical model for describing
the correlation of spatial channel fading in wireless commu-
nications. Its compatibility is exceptionally high with FA port
structures featuring dense arrangements and minimal spacing.
In the literature, [2], [5]±[7] have used this model for analysis.
The Nakagami-m channel employs a parameterization
method using the sum of squares of m Rayleigh random vari-
ables. A single correlated Nakagami-m channel is generated
by m×L complex Gaussian random variables with zero mean
and variance 1/2. To better represent the channel coefﬁcient
˜g

D. Analysis of Receiver Quality in FA-RIS-NOMA Systems
In FA, the user side generally selects the port with the largest
channel gain to receive the signal. It is reasonable to deﬁne
the matrix representation of the cascaded channel as
h
eq , il

ˆ

N×N
, with ε
n
(n = 1, . . . , N ) being
the phase shift introduced by the n-th element of RIS, which
is the same variable as in (2).
Unlike conventional antennas that rely on full CSI for
beamforming, the port selection in FA systems primarily
depends on the channel magnitude information of each port.
FAs rapidly scan and measure the received signal strength
across multiple ports. This allows them to acquire real-time
channel gain information locally. This approach is suitable for
massive antenna or high-frequency communication scenarios
[34]. Based on this, the system selects the port with the best
instantaneous channel conditions. The selection criterion can
be expressed as:
B

represents the corresponding maximum gain
value.
Considering two users at the receiving end, under the
NOMA principle, the strong user UE
1
equipped with FA
will ﬁrst decode the signal of the weak user UE
2
, and then
recover its own signal through SIC. In this paper, the outage
probability is chosen to characterize the performance metrics
of the system.
Assuming that the FA activates the optimal port, which
has the highest signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR)
given by

Then, after passing through SIC, the interference in the

2
is eliminated, and ultimately only the
signal itself needs to be decoded. Therefore, the received SNR
at UE

Finally, UE

decode its signal directly. However, decoding
the signal of UE
1
requires high SINR, which typically exceeds
UE
2
’s channel capability. Since UE
2
cannot perform SIC, the
signal from UE

is treated as inherent interference. Therefore,

E. Modeling of Practical Imperfections
1) Imperfect SIC: While theoretical derivations typically
assume perfect SIC (pSIC) for UE
1
, in practical scenarios,
complete cancellation of the signal from UE
2
is often unattain-
able due to decoding errors or complexity constraints. To
align with the realistic settings in the simulation section, we
model the residual interference as a linear fraction of the
interfering signal power. Consequently, under imperfect SIC
(ipSIC) conditions, the SINR for UE
1
to decode its own signal
is modiﬁed as
γ
ipSIC
UE
1
=
eγP
1
B
∗
1
d
−α
BR
d
−α
1
βeγP
2
B
∗
1
d
−α
BR
d
−α
1
+ 1
, (16)
where β (0 ≤ β ≤ 1) denotes the residual interference
coefﬁcient.
When β = 0, this expression reduces to the pSIC case. It
is worth noting that for UE
2
, the SINR expression remains
unchanged, as it directly treats the signal from UE
1
as inter-
ference without the need to perform SIC operations.
2) Imperfect CSI: Accurate acquisition of CSI is chal-
lenging in dynamic wireless environments. To capture this
practical constraint, we introduce estimation errors based on
the cascaded channel h
eq , il
in (11). Speciﬁcally, the estimated
cascaded channel for the i -th user at the l-th port, denoted as
ˆ
h
eq , il
, is modeled as
ˆ
h
eq , il
= h
eq , il
+ e
il
, (17)
where e
il
∼ CN(0, ξ) denotes the cascaded channel estimation
error with variance ξ.
This error introduces residual interference during the SIC
process at UE
1
, the power of which is directly related to ξ.
Accordingly, under imperfect CSI (iCSI) conditions, the SINR
of UE
1
can be expressed by
γ
iCSI
UE
1
=
eγP
1
B
∗
1
d
−α
BR
d
−α
1
eγP
2
ξ + 1
, (18)
where the term ˜γP
2
ξ represents the equivalent interference
power caused by channel estimation errors.
It is worth noting that for UE
2
, while iCSI may result in
suboptimal port selection, the mathematical structure of its
SINR expression remains consistent with the ideal case.
3) Hardware Impairments: In addition to channel estima-
tion errors, practical transceivers are susceptible to physical
hardware impairments (HWI), such as phase noise and power
ampliﬁer non-linearities. To evaluate their impact on system
performance, we model these impairments as additive Gaus-
sian distortion noise.
For UE
1
, the distortion noise generated by HWI cannot be
eliminated through the SIC technique. Consequently, when
decoding its own signal, the SINR denominator includes
this residual distortion term. Accordingly, its SINR can be
expressed as
γ
HWI
UE
1
=
eγP
1

1
eγκ

∗

d
−α
BR
d
−α
1
+ 1
, (19)
where κ is the HWI coefﬁcient, representing the proportional
relationship between the distortion noise power and the re-
ceived signal power.

III. PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS
In this section, the outage probability of the NOMA users in
the proposed scheme will be discussed and analyzed, and the
outage probability expressions for each user will be derived.
A. Statistical Characteristics
In consideration of the number of RIS elements, ideal phase
shifts, optimal port selection in FA, port correlation, and SIC
in NOMA, the following analysis of the outage probability is
performed.
Suppose that for each port l at the i-th user, the sum of the
amplitude products of each link after passing through the RIS
element is denoted by C

To select the optimal port in an FA system, the cascaded
channel gain must be calculated for each port. This is essential
to overcome user-end fading and maximize the signal strength.
This computation forms a critical system operation. Consider-
ing the independent distribution characteristics of the BS-RIS
and RIS-UE links, this gain depends on the combined effect
of the channel gains of the two links. Speciﬁcally, under the
condition of ideal phase alignment in the RIS, the cascaded
channel gain at port can be approximated as shown in (22):

m

We follow the mathematical transformation of the CDF.
Considering the special nature of the optimal port in FA, and
the CDF of the optimal port is
F
E
∗
eq,i
(x) = P

max

E
1
eq , i

eq , i

≤ x

E
1
eq,i
,...,E
L
eq,i
(x, . . . , x). (24)
Under the above deﬁnition, this paper characterizes the
multivariate joint distribution of L dependent random vari-
ables E
l
eq , i
(where l ∈ {1, ..., L}). However, for correlated
Nakagami-m fading channels, deriving the exact closed-form
joint CDF directly is mathematically intractable. To address
this, we employ the Gaussian copula approach. The preference
for the Gaussian copula in this context is grounded in its ability
to decouple the marginal distributions from the dependence
structure. It allows us to construct a valid joint distribution
using only the marginals and the correlation matrix, thereby
offering a tractable analytical framework to accurately capture
the spatial dependency among FA ports.
To construct such a distribution, we adopt Sklar’s theo-
rem [36]. This theorem enables the construction of a joint
distribution by connecting marginal distributions through a
copula function. Speciﬁcally, the Gaussian copula can ef-
fectively characterize the linear correlation structure of the
spatially dependent random variables E
l
eq , i
in this paper. In the
RIS-aided spatially correlated Nakagami-m fading scenario,
multipath propagation and spatial correlation between ports
can be approximately modeled by the correlation matrix of
the Gaussian copula.
Based on this theorem, function F
E
∗
eq,i
can be expressed as
F

∗
eq,i
(x) = Ψ

for both the strong and weak users in the NOMA system.

for instantaneous SNR is

IV. GROUP OPTIMIZATION GREEDY DETECTION
A. Introduction to GPGD Detection
In conventional approaches, FA systems typically activate
only the single port with the highest instantaneous channel
gain, prioritizing implementation simplicity. However, this
method forfeits the inherent diversity gain available from other
ports, making the system vulnerable to deep fades and perfor-
mance degradation in adverse channel conditions. Therefore,
this paper proposes a novel strategy of selecting and combining
signals from multiple ports to enhance overall reliability and
interference resistance. However, using ML detection would
result in an exponential increase in computational complex-
ity due to the increased number of ports. To address this,
a grouping method is adopted, which signiﬁcantly reduces
computational complexity while retaining key performance
advantages of multi-port reception such as diversity gain and
interference resilience.
This article uses two ports as examples. The port selection
strategy for these is described as follows:
The ﬁrst port selection method is to take the maximum value
of the L-port cascaded channel gain for the i-th user, that is
B
1
= max{B
i1
, B
i2
, . . . , B
iL
}. (42)
The second port selection is to take the maximum gain value
after excluding the ﬁrst port position, that is
B
2
= max ({B
i1
, B
i2
, . . . , B
iL
} \ {B
1
}) . (43)
It is not difﬁcult to see that both ports still use exhaus-
tive search during the selection process, and the complexity
remains high. Therefore, this section develops a GPGD to
address the high complexity issue.
To reduce port correlation, ports are grouped inspired by
[37]. Within each group, port correlation remains unchanged.
Meanwhile, inter-group correlation becomes lower than in
ungrouped conﬁgurations.
In this paper, each FA is equipped with L ports, divided
into G groups where each group contains L
G
=

L
G

ports. It

(41)
should be noted that when the total number of ports cannot be
evenly divided, the last group may contain fewer than L
G
ports, denoted as L
(G)
G
≤ L
G
. Since only one valid port
needs to be selected from each group, such difference does
not require special handling. Within each group, port selection
is based on energy detection, that is, cascaded channel gain,
to determine the optimal port in each group. The calculation
method involves selecting the port with the highest channel
gain within each group.
The following is the design concept: Deﬁne the port index
set of group a as
R
a
= {(a − 1)L
G
+ 1, (a − 1)L
G
+ 2, . . . , min(aL
G
, L) },
(44)
where a = 1, 2, . . . , G.
Each group is based on the cascaded channel gains B
il
=
|h
eq , il
|
2
. So the optimal port index position k
a
within the
group and the corresponding gain value K
max,a
are deter-
mined by the following equation:
k
a
= arg max

a
|B

|, (45)
K
max,a
= max
l∈R
a
|B
il
|, (46)
where the value of k
a
is an integer in the port index set R
a
of that group.
After traversing all groups, a candidate set Q is generated.
This set contains the best ports from each group to enhance
interference resistance and mitigate single-port outage risks,
as shown below.
Q = {k
1
, k
2
, . . . , k
G
}, (47)
where k
X
{X ∈ (1, . . . , G)} is the best port index position
corresponding to group X.
The ﬁnal port location is selected from the candidate set Q
by choosing the two ports with the highest gains.
k

= arg max

k

|. (49)
Speciﬁcally, the computational complexity of ML detec-
tion and GPGD is measured by the number of real number
multiplications during the detection process. The complexity
calculations for both are shown in Table II, where b =

B+2

N
r
N
2
+ N
r
N

The main difference between the two methods in Table II
lies in how the two ports are selected. ML detection directly
activates two ports from all physical ports, while GPGD ﬁrst
ﬁlters a set of best port candidates and then selects two ports
from this candidate set for activation. The most notable feature
of the grouping strategy is its ability to effectively reduce
system complexity. To clearly compare the computational
complexity of the two methods, this paper varies both G and
L, and compares the complexity of the two approaches under
different conﬁgurations. The results are presented in Table III
and Table IV.
TABLE III
COMPLEXITY COMPARISON OF ML AND GPGD FOR DIFFERENT G
L G ML GPGD Reduction Percentage
100 10 2.38 × 10
6
2.16 × 10
4
99.04%
100 25 2.38 × 10
6
1.44 × 10
5
93.94%
100 50 2.38 × 10
6
5.88 × 10
5
75.25%
As shown in Table III, the ML-based detection exhaustively
selects two optimal ports from all available FA ports, requiring
a full traversal of elements. Its computational complexity is
ﬁxed and independent of the number of G. In contrast, the
complexity of the GPGD depends on the number of G: it
increases with G, yet remains substantially lower than that of
the ML detection across all conﬁgurations.
According to Table IV, under the premise that the number
of ﬁxed groups is set to 25, as the number of ports L increases
from 50 to 200. The complexity of ML detection rises sharply

9
TABLE IV
COMPLEXITY COMPARISON OF ML AND GPGD FOR DIFFERENT L
G L ML GPGD Reduction Percentage
25 50 5.88 × 10
5
1.44 × 10
5
75.51%
25 100 2.38 × 10
6
1.44 × 10
5
93.94%
25 200 9.55 × 10
6
1.44 × 10
5
98.49%
(since it needs to traverse all port combinations), increasing
from 5.88 × 10
5
to 9.55 × 10
6
. In contrast, the GPGD only
needs to process group combinations, so its complexity re-
mains stably at 1.44×10
5
. This leads to a substantial increase
in the percentage reduction of computational complexity as
L becomes larger: it decreases by 75.51% when L = 50,
reaches 93.94% when L = 100, and reaches to 98.49% when
L = 200. The results show that in communication systems, the
GPGD can suppress the growth of complexity more efﬁciently
through its group processing mechanism. Its advantage is
particularly prominent in large-scale port scenarios.
B. Multi user extension
To accommodate massive IoT connectivity, the proposed
GPGD algorithm can be extended to E users via a user-pairing
strategy. Speciﬁcally, all users are ﬁrst sorted in descending
order based on their optimal port channel gains:
B
∗(1)
> B
∗(2)
> ··· > B
∗(E−1)
> B
∗(E)
, (50)
where B
∗(i)
denotes the maximum channel gain of the i-th
sorted user.
Subsequently, users are grouped into C =
E
2
pairs using
a ªStrongest-Weakest User Pairing (SW-UP)º criterion, where
the d-th pair (d = 1, . . . , C) consists of the d-th strong user
and the (E−d+1)-th weak user. In this setup, the BS transmits
superposed signals for each pair. The transmitted signal for the
d-th pair is given by:
s
d
=
p
P
d,S
x
d,S
+
p
P
d,W
x
d,W
, (51)
where P
d,S
and P
d,W
represent the power allocation factors
for the strong and weak users in the d-th pair, satisfying P
d,S
+
P
d,W
= 1.
At the receiver side, the GPGD algorithm operates indepen-
dently for each user to select the optimal ports. The strong
user employs SIC to cancel the weak user’s signal before
decoding its own, while the weak user treats the strong user’s
signal as interference. Since the port selection and detection
are distributed, the computational complexity scales linearly
with E, making the proposed framework highly suitable for
large-scale IoT networks.
V. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
A. Simulation Setup
This section demonstrates the advantages of the proposed
FA-RIS-NOMA system over existing ones. Simulation results
primarily analyze outage probability using Monte Carlo meth-
ods, while also validating the theoretically derived outage
expressions. Here, we set path loss coefﬁcient α = 2.1,
Nakagami-m fading parameter m = 2, target rate ν
1
= ν
2
=
1.9 bps/Hz for both the strong and weak users, number of
elements N = 5 in the RIS, and size W = 2 of the FA.
B. Outage Performance Analysis
0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45

Theoretical
Fig. 2. Comparison of FA and ﬁxed position antenna (FPA) system outage
probability performance.
Fig. 2 primarily compares the impact of FA on the system.
For the traditional ﬁxed antenna model referenced in [33], to
ensure the fairness of the experiment, the RIS-UE channel
was also replaced with a Nakagami-m channel. As shown in
Fig. 2, the overall trend of the outage probability decreases
gradually with the increase in SNR. In particular, when users
are equipped with FA, its performance is signiﬁcantly better
than that of traditional antennas. Especially at high SNR levels,
the FA achieves an outage probability of nearly 10
−3
at 27
dB, while the traditional ﬁxed antenna requires the SNR of
36 dB to achieve the same outage probability. This is largely
due to the FA’s ability to selectively activate the best port
from multiple ports, thereby gaining selection diversity gain.
Furthermore, whether using FA or FPA, the performance of
UE
1
is better than that of UE
2
. This is because UE
1
utilizes
SIC more extensively, providing a higher SINR compared
to UE
2
. The results derived from the theoretical formulas
(36) and (41) in Section III, along with the Monte Carlo
simulations, are presented in Fig. 2. Overall, the theoretical
and simulated curves show strong agreement, validating the
analytical model. However, the Gaussian copula constitutes
an approximation of the actual channel correlation structure.
In scenarios with strong port correlation, the ﬁtting error of
the copula becomes more pronounced, leading to a discernible
discrepancy between the theoretical and simulated results,
particularly in the high-SNR region.
Fig. 3 further investigates the impact of channel fading
severity on system performance and speciﬁcally validates the
effectiveness of the Gaussian copula-based theoretical model
under various Nakagami-m shape parameters m. It can be
observed that as m increases from 0.5 to 2.0, representing a
transition from severe to milder fading conditions, the outage
probabilities for all users exhibit a signiﬁcant downward
trend. Additionally, the performance of UE
1
is consistently
superior to that of UE
2
, beneﬁting from the SIC process. By
comparing the theoretical calculations with simulation results,

Fig. 3. Impact of different Nakagami-m fading parameters on system outage
probability.
it is found that the theoretically derived outage probability
curves demonstrate good agreement with the simulations for
m = 1.0 and m = 2.0. This veriﬁes the feasibility of
employing the Gaussian copula method to model spatially
correlated FA ports. It is worth noting that when m = 0.5, the
theoretical and simulation curves exhibit a visible discrepancy.
This deviation reﬂects the limitation of the Gaussian copula
in capturing the dependence between deep fading events
when dealing with severe fading distributions characterized
by strong non-Gaussian features. In summary, the proposed
approximation model demonstrates high accuracy in typical
urban multipath environments and RIS-enhanced scenarios
where m ≥ 1. Furthermore, for severe fading scenarios with
0.5 ≤ m < 1, the method remains effective in characterizing
the overall variation trend of system performance, thereby
providing valuable theoretical references for system design.

2
=0.8, L=15)
Fig. 4. Impact of FA port density on system outage performance.
To validate the performance of the model under varying
spatial correlations, Fig. 4 illustrates the impact of FA port
density on system performance and theoretical approximation
accuracy. With a ﬁxed antenna size W , the channel environ-
ment variation from sparse to dense distribution is simulated
by increasing the number of ports L from 5 to 15. It can be
observed that as L increases, the spatial diversity gain achieved
by the system gradually improves, leading to enhanced outage
performance. When the number of ports L is 5, the variation
trends of the theoretical curves remain consistent with the
simulation results; as L increases to 10, a good agreement
is exhibited between the two without any crossover. Upon
further observation, as L increases to 15, although the overall
ﬁt remains high, the theoretical and simulation curves exhibit a
crossover phenomenon in certain SNR regions due to the sig-
niﬁcantly enhanced spatial correlation caused by the reduced
port spacing. This phenomenon intuitively reveals the limita-
tion of the Gaussian copula method. Speciﬁcally, when deal-
ing with high-density and strongly correlated non-Gaussian
channels, the Gaussian copula’s dependence structure struggles
to accurately capture the complex tail dependence within
the actual channel distribution. Consequently, this leads to
local ﬂuctuations of the theoretical approximations in speciﬁc
regions. Therefore, despite the existence of marginal ﬁtting
deviations in high-density scenarios, the theoretical model can
still correctly reﬂect the performance gain trends induced by
increasing the number of ports. Thus, it provides signiﬁcant
reference value for evaluating the performance upper bounds
and variation laws of multi-port systems.

,P

=0.3)

1
,P

=0.3)
FA-Relay-NOMA (UE
2
,P
2
=0.7)
8dB
8dB
Fig. 5. Performance comparison between relay users and RIS-assisted users.
In NOMA technology research, BS and UE often use
repeaters to amplify and forward signals to extend coverage
or improve performance. Notably, some studies, such as [12],
introduce relay users as signal mediators between BS and
target UEs. To thoroughly evaluate performance differences,
this paper directly compares the relay user system model
described in [12] with the proposed FA-RIS-NOMA system.
From Fig. 5, we can observe that the FA-RIS-NOMA scheme
signiﬁcantly outperforms the relay user scheme, with an ad-
vantage of approximately 8 dB. Such signiﬁcant performance
improvement stems from the unique physical characteristics
of RIS. Especially in NLOS environments, RIS effectively
compensates for channel phase offsets in both BS-RIS and
RIS-UE links through reﬂection unit phase optimization. Ad-
This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Internet of Things Journal. This is the author's version which has not been fully edited and
content may change prior to final publication. Citation information: DOI 10.1109/JIOT.2026.3668981
© 2026 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted,
but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 30,2026 at 07:05:46 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
11
ditionally, it optimizes reﬂection paths to mitigate severe path
loss. This active control capability over the wireless channel is
completely unattainable in the relay user scheme. More crit-
ically, relay user schemes operating as relays inevitably pro-
duce strong self-interference signals. This interference arises
during concurrent signal reception and retransmission pro-
cesses. Such inherent self-interference severely degrades the
reception quality of the target signal, becoming a performance
bottleneck. In contrast, as a passive reﬂective surface, the
RIS passively reﬂects incident signals, fundamentally avoiding
self-interference issues and ensuring superior signal reception
performance.

15dB
15dB
Fig. 6. Impact of the number of RIS elements on system performance.
Fig. 6 shows a comparison of the outage probability perfor-
mance of UE
1
and UE
2
under different RIS unit conﬁgura-
tions. The simulation results clearly demonstrate that increas-
ing the number of RIS units yields signiﬁcant performance
gains. In particular, when the number of RIS units increases
from 5 to 25, the system performance improvement approaches
15 dB, resulting in a highly signiﬁcant performance gain.
Additionally, as the SNR improves, the outage probability for
both UE
1
and UE
2
decreases, enhancing overall communica-
tion reliability. The core advantage resides in RIS-provided
spatial diversity capability. Under deep fading or obstacle-
induced signal weakening conditions, RIS units intelligently
regulate reﬂected signals to maintain communication links,
thereby fundamentally enhancing cascaded channel quality.
Therefore, such result demonstrates that increasing RIS units
signiﬁcantly enhances wireless channel control freedom. The
added units concentrate reﬂected signal energy and opti-
mize transmission paths. Consequently, the proposed FA-RIS-
NOMA scheme achieves substantial SNR improvement and
enhances network coverage reliability at the receiver.
Fig. 7 illustrates the outage probability versus the SNR for
RIS-NOMA and FA-RIS-NOMA systems under different SIC
conditions. The considered cases include pSIC and ipSIC, with
the ipSIC coefﬁcient β set to 0.01 and 0.05, respectively. The
results show that as SNR increases, the outage probability of
both systems decreases signiﬁcantly. Speciﬁcally, the pSIC
scheme achieves the best outage probability performance,
while the ipSIC scheme’s outage probability performance
varies with β Ð the smaller β is, the closer its performance
is to pSIC. It is worth noting that only the SIC mechanism for
UE
1
is analyzed, and UE
2
’s performance curve is unaffected
by β changes as it does not involve SIC operations. Comparing
RIS-NOMA and FA-RIS-NOMA systems, FA-RIS-NOMA
demonstrates superior outage probability performance due to
the spatial diversity gain enabled by the multi-port feature
of FA, fully highlighting its potential in enhancing system
transmission reliability.

Fig. 8. Impact of iCSI on outage probability.
Fig. 8 illustrates the impact of iCSI on the system’s outage
probability. It can be observed that the outage probabil-
ity generally decreases as the SNR increases. However, the
presence of channel estimation error ξ introduces an error
ﬂoor. This impact is particularly pronounced for UE
1
, which
performs SIC. As ξ increases from 0.002 to 0.010, the outage
probability curve for UE
1
rises rapidly and exhibits a distinct
This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Internet of Things Journal. This is the author's version which has not been fully edited and
content may change prior to final publication. Citation information: DOI 10.1109/JIOT.2026.3668981

12
ﬂoor, indicating that its performance is severely limited by
residual interference from SIC. In contrast, UE
2
, which does
not require SIC, is less affected by ξ, showing a relatively
moderate performance degradation. These results reveal the
extreme sensitivity of UE
1
to CSI accuracy in NOMA systems
and verify the critical role of high-quality channel estimation
in ensuring SIC performance.

Fig. 9. Impact of HWI on outage probability.
In addition to the iCSI, we further evaluate the impact of
physical HWI. Fig. 9 depicts the outage probability variations
under different distortion levels κ. Unlike the error ﬂoor
induced by channel estimation errors, HWI manifests primarily
as a horizontal SNR penalty. In the low SNR region, addi-
tive noise masks these impairments, resulting in overlapping
curves. Conversely, in the high SNR regime, the aggregate dis-
tortion becomes dominant, causing a noticeable rightward shift
in the performance curves. Speciﬁcally, the outage probability
performance of UE
1
deteriorates more signiﬁcantly compared
to UE
2
, primarily due to the error propagation inherent in the
SIC process. Nevertheless, a key distinction is that the curves
maintain a downward trajectory without saturation, implying
that the system can tolerate hardware imperfections given
sufﬁcient transmit power.
In Fig. 10, FA has different effects on the system at different
sizes, and an interesting phenomenon emerges. As the size
increases from W = 0.5 to W = 2, the impact of size on the
performance of UE
1
and UE
2
is signiﬁcant, with performance
improves slightly as the size increases. This occurs because,
for a ﬁxed number of ports, larger dimensions increase the
inter-port distance, reducing spatial correlation and enhancing
system performance. However, when the size reaches a certain
threshold, such as W = 5, the system performance does not
improve signiﬁcantly compared to W = 2. As mentioned in
[2], when the number of ports is large, the differences in size
become negligible, leading to the convergence of performance
as the size increases.
As shown in Fig. 11, under different conﬁgurations of RIS

Fig. 11. Comparison of ML and GPGD outage probabilities, where L = 50.
element numbers, the performance of the outage probability
based on the GPGD and the ML detection (selecting the two
best ports) are highly consistent. Especially when the number
of RIS elements increases to 10, the difference in outage
probability between the two schemes remains within 0.5 dB.
Furthermore, Table III and IV experimentally demonstrate the
efﬁciency of the GPGD algorithm. This approach signiﬁcantly
reduces computational complexity via a grouping mechanism
while maintaining near-ML performance. Such result validates
that the grouping multi-port selection scheme ensures feasibil-
ity at the receiver end, providing an effective low-complexity
solution for multi-port systems.
To validate the scalability of the proposed GPGD algorithm
in massive IoT scenarios, we extend the simulations to an
8-user pairing scenario. As shown in Fig. 12, NOMA-based
schemes consistently outperform OMA, verifying the spec-
tral efﬁciency beneﬁts of the architecture. Regarding pairing
schemes, with only 2 users (E = 2), the performance curves
for Random Pairing and SW-UP overlap completely. This
is because, with only a single pair of users available, the

Fig. 12. Comparison of SW-UP, Random Pairing, and OMA under different
numbers of users.
pairing outcome is deterministic regardless of the selection
strategy. However, as the network scales to E = 8, the SW-UP
strategy exhibits a discernible performance improvement over
Random Pairing. This observed improvement is attributed to
the speciﬁc user-pairing strategy, where the d-th strongest user
is paired with the (E −d +1)-th weakest user based on sorted
channel gains, thereby avoiding suboptimal pairings. These
results indicate that the GPGD algorithm, when combined
with channel-aware pairing, can effectively support multi-user
expansion without compromising system reliability.
C. Throughput and BER Analysis

1
(SNR=15dB),P

Fig. 13. Throughput comparison for different SNR with modulation order
M = 4.
Throughput is deﬁned as the amount of error-free data suc-
cessfully transmitted per unit of time, serving as a key metric
for evaluating the effective transmission capacity of a system.
The simulation results in Fig. 13 strongly validate the theoret-
ical expectations and performance advantages of the FA-RIS-
NOMA system shown in Fig. 2. Speciﬁcally, at the SNR of
20 dB, UE
1
’s outage probability signiﬁcantly decreases due
to the system design, directly driving an effective increase
in its throughput. In sharp contrast, UE
2
’s outage probability
remains at 1 at this SNR, with its throughput remaining at 0.
When the SNR is increased to 30 dB, the system performance
is further optimized. The outage probability of UE
1
can be
reduced to 10
−3
, while the outage probability of UE
2
also
improves signiﬁcantly to 10
−1.8
. Increasing the number of
ports L signiﬁcantly enhances the system’s spatial diversity
gain and beamforming capabilities. This enhancement causes
a continuous decrease in both users’ outage probabilities.
Concurrently, their throughputs converge toward the respective
target rates v
i
. This fully demonstrates the superior potential of
the FA-RIS-NOMA architecture in enhancing fairness among
users at different locations and overall system throughput
efﬁciency.
-10 -5 0 5 10 15 20 25 30
SNR (dB)
10
-4

BER
UE
1
(N=5, P

=0.2)
UE
2
(N=5, P

1
(N=10, P

1

2
=0.8)
Fig. 14. Comparison of BER performance with different numbers of RIS and
M = 4.
Fig. 14 shows a comparison of BER performance of UE
1
and UE
2
in the system under different numbers of RIS units.
Simulation results indicate that as the number of RIS units
increases, the BER performance of both users improves over-
all. Additionally, a critical crossover phenomenon is observed
across all RIS conﬁgurations: the BER performance curves
of the two users exhibit a distinct crossover point. In the
lower SNR region to the left of the intersection point, UE
2
’s
BER performance is slightly better than UE
1
’s. In the higher
SNR region to the right of the intersection point, UE
1
’s
BER performance is signiﬁcantly better than UE
2
’s. This
phenomenon stems from the inherent power allocation strategy
of NOMA and its dynamic interaction with channel conditions.
Speciﬁcally, in the region to the left of the intersection point
(noise-dominated region), NOMA assigns a higher transmis-
sion power coefﬁcient to UE
2
. This enables UE
2
to achieve a
relatively higher receive gain at low SNR. Thus, it temporarily
gains a performance advantage. However, as SNR increases to
the right of the intersection point, UE
1
leverages its superior
This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Internet of Things Journal. This is the author's version which has not been fully edited and
content may change prior to final publication. Citation information: DOI 10.1109/JIOT.2026.3668981
© 2026 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted,
but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 30,2026 at 07:05:46 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
14
channel conditions. It also effectively mitigates interference
from UE
2
through the SIC process at the receiver end. This
allows UE
1
to rapidly improve its received SNR and ultimately
overtake UE
2
. Despite this local crossover phenomenon, the
overall system trend across different numbers of RIS is clear.
As the SNR increases, UE
1
beneﬁts from superior channel
conditions and effective SIC, ultimately achieving more stable
error rate performance than UE
2
. This fully aligns with
the theoretical expectations of NOMA system design, deeply
revealing the synergistic mechanism between power allocation
and channel conditions in a dynamic SNR environment under
RIS assistance.
D. Key Findings and Limitations
The proposed FA-RIS-NOMA framework demonstrates
substantial robustness and efﬁciency, effectively overcoming
the limitations of conventional architectures. Speciﬁcally, the
integration of FA yields an SNR gain of approximately
9 dB over FPA, while the RIS deployment outperforms relay
schemes by roughly 8 dB by mitigating self-interference.
Channel control is further strengthened by increasing RIS
elements, providing nearly 15 dB gain when scaling from 5 to
25 units. Under non-ideal conditions, HWI and ipSIC lead to
varying degrees of performance degradation in the high-SNR
regime, while channel estimation errors are particularly critical
for UE
1
. Additionally, the diversity gain from FA sizing is
shown to saturate once the normalized size exceeds W = 5.
In terms of algorithmic efﬁcacy and system capacity, the
designed GPGD strategy signiﬁcantly reduces computational
complexity while maintaining outage probabilities within
0.5 dB of optimal ML detection, proving its viability for
large-scale port systems. Scalability is further evidenced by
the SW-UP scheme, which consistently outperforms OMA
and Random Pairing methods in multi-user scenarios. More-
over, throughput analysis conﬁrms that increasing antenna
ports drives user rates toward target levels, thereby enhancing
fairness. Finally, BER comparisons exhibit a crossover phe-
nomenon where UE
2
beneﬁts from power allocation at low
SNR, whereas UE
1
dominates at high SNR due to effective
interference cancellation, highlighting the dynamic interplay
between NOMA protocols and channel conditions.
It is worth noting that the superior reliability achieved by
the FA-RIS-NOMA architecture involves a trade-off between
performance and signaling overhead. Speciﬁcally, acquiring
instantaneous CSI for optimal port selection requires pilot
resources, which may marginally impact spectral efﬁciency in
large-scale implementations. Additionally, as indicated by the
simulation results, residual HWI can inﬂuence the performance
ceiling in high-SNR regimes. These practical aspects present
meaningful directions for future robust system designs.
VI. CONCLUSION
In this paper, we investigate signal transmission for users
equipped with FA in a multi-user NOMA environment with
the assistance of RIS. Unlike prior studies, this paper uses a
Nakagami-m channel for the RIS-UE link to ﬂexibly capture
multipath effects in complicated environments. Additionally,
to compare the outage probability performance across users,
this paper derives a multivariate CDF expression based on an
error function under the equivalent channel of FA users in
a single-port selection scenario, and integrates this approach
into Monte Carlo simulations. To reduce receiver complexity,
a low-complexity GPGD is proposed when activating two
ports. Simulation results demonstrate that this method exhibits
low complexity and superior outage probability performance.
Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the pro-
posed scheme under the FA-RIS-NOMA framework.
Numerical results validate the robustness of the FA-RIS-
NOMA architecture, demonstrating SNR gains of approxi-
mately 9 dB and 8 dB over FPA and relay-based benchmarks,
respectively. Furthermore, expanding the RIS scale is shown
to yield a substantial performance boost of nearly 15 dB.
Finally, the proposed GPGD algorithm is proven to balance
efﬁciency and reliability, approximating the optimal detection
bound within a marginal gap of 0.5 dB.
Our future research will explore different multiple access
schemes within this framework, integrating FA technology
into RIS designs to further enhance diversity gain in the
signal transmission process. In addition, index modulation
techniques could also be considered further to improve the
spectral efﬁciency of the proposed scheme.

Haiying Chen received the B.S. degree in Commu-
nication Engineering from Tongji Zhejiang College,
Zhejiang, China, in 2024. He is currently pursuing
the master’s degree with China Jiliang University.
His research interests include wireless communica-
tion technologies, such as multiple access, reconﬁg-
urable intelligent surfaces, and ﬂuid antenna.
Xiaoping Jin (Member, IEEE) received the B.S. and
M.E degree from Zhejiang University of Technology
in 2001 and 2004, Hangzhou, China. She received
the Ph.D. degree from Ningbo University of Tech-
nology in 2018, Ningbo, China. She is currently a
Professor with the College of Information Engineer-
ing, China jiliang University, Hangzhou, China. Her
research interests include wireless communications,
MIMO, index modulation, reconﬁgurable intelligent
surfaces, and orthogonal time frequency space mod-
ulation.
Weidong Chen received the B.E. degree in Com-
puter Science and Technology from China Jiliang
University, Hangzhou, China, in 2006. With more
than 20 years of extensive experience in the telecom-
munications industry, he has amassed 16 years of
specialized R&D experience in 3G, 4G, and 5G
technologies. Proﬁcient in LTE and NR L2/L3 proto-
cols, he possesses in-depth expertise in the full life-
cycle development, optimization, and conformance
testing of mobile communication systems. His core
capabilities cover protocol stack design, functional
module development, performance tuning, and interoperability testing across
diverse 3GPP release versions. Throughout his career, he has led multiple key
R&D projects, delivering reliable technical solutions and high-quality protocol
implementations that meet industry standards.

content may change prior to final publication. Citation information: DOI 10.1109/JIOT.2026.3668981

16
Yao Ge (Member, IEEE) received the Ph.D. degree
in Electronic Engineering from The Chinese Uni-
versity of Hong Kong (CUHK), Shatin, Hong Kong,
in 2021, and the M.Eng. degree (research) in Com-
munication and Information System and the B.Eng.
degree in Electronics and Information Engineering
from Northwestern Polytechnical University (NPU),
Xi’an, China, in 2016 and 2013, respectively. He is
currently a Research Fellow with the AUMOVIO-
NTU Corporate Lab, Nanyang Technological Uni-
versity (NTU), Singapore. From October 2015 to
March 2016, he was a Visiting Scholar with the Department of Electrical
and Computer Systems Engineering, Monash University, Melbourne, VIC,
Australia. From April 2016 to August 2016, he was a Visiting Scholar
with the Department of Computer, Electrical and Mathematical Science
and Engineering, King Abdullah University of Science and Technology
(KAUST), Thuwal, Saudi Arabia. From May 2019 to December 2019, he
was a Visiting Scholar with the Department of Electrical and Computer
Engineering, University of California at Davis (UC Davis), Davis, CA, USA.
His current research interests include wireless communications and system
design, the Internet of Things, cognitive radio networks, automotive vehicle
signal processing and communications, integrated sensing and communication,
wireless network security, statistical signal processing, optimization, and game
theory. He received the Best Paper Award from the International Conference
on Wireless Communications and Signal Processing (WCSP) 2022 and the
Best Poster Award of the Chinese Institute of Electronics Conference on
Information Theory 2024. He is a Founding Member of the IEEE ComSoc
Special Interest Group (SIG) on OTFS and has served as the technical program
committee member/co-chair for numerous international conferences. He is
also a Youth Editor of the Engineering, Journal of Information and Intelligence
and a Regional Editor of the Computing & AI Connect.
Meiyan Song (Member, IEEE) received the B.Eng.
degree in communication engineering from Wuhan
University of Technology, Wuhan, China, in 2018,
and the Ph.D. degree in electrical engineering from
Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, China, in 2023.
She is currently an Associate Professor with the
College of Information Engineering, China Jiliang
University, Hangzhou, China. Her research interests
include the performance analysis and optimization
of wireless networks.
Jianrong Bao (Senior Member, IEEE) received
his Ph.D. degree in Information & Communica-
tion Engineering from the Depart. of Electronic
Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, China,
in 2009. He has been a Postdoctoral Researcher
with Zhejiang University during 2011-2013, and
with Southeast University during 2014-2017, and
then a Visiting Scholar with Columbia University,
New York, NY, USA, in 2015, respectively. He
is currently served as a Professor in the School
of Communication Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi
University, Hangzhou, China. His main research interests include wireless
communications, information theory and coding, wireless sensor network and
so on.
Chongwen Huang (Member, IEEE) obtained his
B.Sc. degree in 2010 from Nankai University, and
the M.Sc degree from the University of Electronic
Science and Technology of China in 2013, and Ph.D
degree from Singapore University of Technology
and Design (SUTD) in 2019. From Oct. 2019 to
Sep. 2020, he is a Postdoc in SUTD. Since Sep.
2020, he joined into Zhejiang University as a tenure-
track young professor. Dr. Huang is the recipient
of 2021 IEEE Marconi Prize Paper Award, 2023
IEEE Fred W. Ellersick Prize Paper Award and
2021 IEEE ComSoc Asia-Paciﬁc Outstanding Young Researcher Award. He
has served as an Editor of IEEE Communications Letter, Elsevier Signal
Processing, EURASIP Journal on Wireless Communications and Networking
and Physical Communication since 2021. His main research interests are
focused on Holographic MIMO Surface/Reconﬁgurable Intelligent Surfaces,
B5G/6G Wireless Communications, mmWave/THz Communications, Deep
Learning technologies for Wireless communications, etc.
Yudong Yao (Fellow, IEEE) received the B.E. and
M.E. degrees in electrical engineering from Nan-
jing University of Posts and Telecommunications,
Nanjing, China, in 1982 and 1985, respectively,
and the Ph.D. degree in electrical engineering from
Southeast University, Nanjing, in 1988. From 1989
to 2000, he was with Carleton University, Ottawa,
ON, Canada; Spar Aerospace Ltd., Montreal, QC,
Canada; and Qualcomm Inc., San Diego, CA, USA.
He is currently a Professor with the Stevens Insti-
tute of Technology, Hoboken, NJ, USA. Prof. His
research interests include wireless communications, cognitive radio, and deep
learning techniques. He was an Associate Editor of the IEEE COMMUNI-
CATIONS LETTERS from 2000 to 2008 and the IEEE TRANSACTIONS
ON VEHICULAR TECHNOLOGY from 2001 to 2006 and an Editor for
the IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON WIRELESS COMMUNICATIONS from
2001 to 2005. Yao was elected as a Fellow of IEEE in 2011, the National
Academy of Inventors in 2015, the Canadian Academy of Engineering in
2017, American Institute for Medical and Biological Engineering in 2021,
and Asia-Paciﬁc Artiﬁcial Intelligence Association in 2022.
